title: Who Mismanages Student Loans, and Why?
abstract: Many financially distressed students who qualify for federal assistance plans with interest moratorium and principal forgiveness instead accrue interest over long periods of nonpayment. This loan mismanagement is associated with higher delinquency. Mismanagement varies significantly across student gender and race: it is more prominent among male and non-white students. Mismanagement also varies across loan servicers, depending on proxies for student-adverse servicer policies. We consider explanations based on student selection and servicer treatment for loan mismanagement. Student financial literacy plays an important role but variation in treatment on the part of loan servicers appears more important.

article.1 Data:
JEL D14 - Household Saving; Personal Finance H52 - Government Expenditures and Education H81 - Governmental Loans; Loan Guarantees; Credits; Grants; Bailouts I22 - Educational Finance; Financial Aid I28 - Government Policy Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

We document and shed light on a puzzle in student loan management. Many financially distressed student borrowers who qualify for generous federal assistance plans with interest moratoriums and principal forgiveness (Income Driven Repaymentplans or loandeferment) instead continue to accrue interest over long periods of nonpayment in loanforbearance.1The accrued interest capitalized during loan forbearance commonly results in future loan balances that exceed the original amount borrowed. Unsurprisingly then, loan forbearance is associated with higher delinquency rates compared to IDR plans or loan deferment, controlling for observable risk factors. We therefore refer to repeated rounds of forbearance by students qualified for IDR plans or deferment as loan “mismanagement.” We examine which students mismanage their loans and explore possible explanations for this puzzle.

Using information on student loan borrowers from the U.S. Department of Education (DOE) Institute of Education Sciences (IES), we identify financially distressed students burdened by significant accrued interest over long periods of loan forbearance and observe whether they then switch to economically advantageous alternatives. Of these long-distressed students, more than 85% accrue additional interest (without hope of principal forgiveness) over continued forbearance rather than switch to IDR plans or deferment. This loan mismanagement varies across student gender and race: female students are about 15% more likely than males to switch loans out of long-term forbearance into IDR plans or deferment; white students are about 8% more likely than non-whites to switch. This variation persists after accounting for loan balance, student earnings, and prospective income growth affecting repayment decisions (Karamcheva, Perry, and Yannelis 2020). Financial literacy—captured by students’ knowledge about fundamental financial concepts, education, field of expertise, and parental background—partially explains variation in loan management; however, it does not subsume the variation by gender or race.

To explain the existence of and variation in student loan mismanagement, we consider the incentives of financial institutions that service student loans. In January 2017, the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) filed legal action against one of the largest student loan servicers alleging that servicer steers troubled borrowers into forbearance away from eligible IDR plans against their best interest. Similar allegations are filed by Attorneys General of several states and class action lawsuits (seeInternet Appendix B). Potential servicer conflicts of interest arises because IDR plans stop interest accrual and offer principal forgiveness; for subsidized loans, only loan forbearance capitalizes interest indefinitely, which in turn extends the duration of the loan thus extending servicer income.2Motivated by these legal complaints, we investigate treatment effects of loan servicers as a possible explanation for student loan mismanagement.

The assignment of servicers administrated by the DOE follows a process largely exogenous to borrowers. As an empirical verification, we observe no significant differences in student demographics across servicers. Yet the observed switching rate out of long-term forbearance varies substantially across servicers from less than 2% (Navient) to 17% (Great Lakes). Switching rates across servicers also varyconditional onborrower demographics. For example, the switching rate across servicers ranges from 3% to 28% for female borrowers and from 1% to 27% for white borrowers. Because the DOE assigns loans similarly across servicers, in terms of borrower characteristics, such variation in switching more likely reflects servicer treatment effects than borrower preferences.

We perform Oaxaca-Blinder decompositions and contrast the extent to which borrower characteristics and loan servicer treatment explain the variation in switching rates. We capture student-adverse servicer policies using borrower complaints against loan servicers filed with the CFPB followingBegley and Purnanandam (2021). Because the CFPB publishes these complaints to protect consumers from egregious practice, the intensity of complaints should correlate with servicer willingness to treat borrowers against their best interest. We find that between Navient and Great Lakes, the proportion of difference in switching rates explained by gender and race is approximately zero. Borrower financial literacy explains about 2% of the switching rate difference. In contrast, servicer policies (complaint intensity) explain over 70% of this variation. We observe similar patterns between other pairs of servicers.

We explore whether servicer treatment further explains the demographic variation in loan mismanagement. Inspired byEgan, Matvos, and Seru (2022), who document gender- and race-based in-group favoritism in the financial adviser industry, we consider the possibility that customer service representatives (“servicer reps”) execute student-adverse servicer policies differently across borrowers.3Because students cannot select their servicer or the servicer rep who answers their call, the random draw of servicer reps likely influences outcome.

Using LinkedIn data, we find that servicer reps are primarily female and white, and they seem more likely to assist distressed borrowers who are likewise female and white, helping them switch out of costly long-term forbearance into better options. Specifically, we observe that female borrowers are particularly more likely to switch into either IDR plans or deferment when they call servicer reps who are also likely female (proxied for by servicers’ employee demographic composition). This is especially the case when female borrowers have a low level of financial literacy (i.e., when information from servicer reps is most valuable). Similarly, white borrowers are more likely to switch when their servicer reps are likely white, though the effect is not as strong as the case of gender-based pairing. These results are consistent with in-group favoritism, adding to prior studies showing that gender and race pairing affects financial outcomes (e.g.,Ambrose, Conklin, and Lopez 2021;Ewens and Townsend 2020;Duchin, Simutin, and Sosyura 2021).4However, because we do not directly observe the identities or demographics of servicer reps interacting with our sample borrowers, we interpret these results as suggestive, acknowledging that they may not fully explain the demographic variation in loan management. Our suggestive evidence calls for future examination with more granular and direct data.

Next, we consider selection-based explanations for student loan mismanagement. Prior literature has documented suboptimal financial management among U.S. households in other contexts, such as failure to maximize employer-matched 401(k) contributions (Choi, Laibson, and Madrian 2011), suboptimal mortgage decisions (Agarwal, Ben-David, and Yao 2017;Andersen et al. 2020), and that finance professionals profit from suboptimal household investment decisions (Egan 2019). We therefore consider the possibility that households similarly mismanage student loans due to unrealistic expectations, high discount rates, or nonpecuniary personal preferences, and that such expectations and preferences might correlate with demographic characteristics. Specifically, we consider the role of student aversion to administrative paperwork suggested by Mueller and Yannelis (2022) and show that it does not explain the observed demographic variation. We also consider borrowers’ consumption preferences and overconfidence, motivated by prior literature indicating demographic variation therein.5We find some evidence that males exhibit more overconfidence than females, suggesting selection effects where men more likely mistake long-term hardship as temporary, but find no such effects related to consumption preferences.

Our results are timely given the size and nonperformance in student debt, and should be of interest to the DOE, student borrowers, loan servicers, and policymakers concerned about easing student debt burdens as well as the taxpayers subsidizing federal student loans. Given the recent regulatory effort promoting accessible and manageable IDR plans, our results suggest which students should benefit most from these changes. Given the ongoing DOE scrutiny of servicers’ forbearance use, our results shed light on potential ways to restructure servicer compensation.6More broadly, we expect that the connection between loan servicer incentives and distressed borrower outcomes studied in the student debt market likely extends to other household debt markets, including credit cards and mortgages. To the extent that servicer conflicts of interest present in other debt markets, oversight of servicer behavior may improve borrower outcomes.
The National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) is the part of the IES that collects education statistics and conducts the Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS). The BPS surveys a representative sample of students who begin postsecondary education during particular years at eligible institutions in the United States. Institutions eligible for the BPS sample are required to meet all criteria for federal aid authorized under Title IV of the Higher Education Act (20 U.S.C. 1070-1099). Students eligible for the BPS sample are enrolled in (1) an academic program, (2) at least one course for credit that could be applied toward an academic degree, or (3) an occupational or vocational program requiring at least 3 months or 300 hours of instruction to receive a degree, certificate, or other formal award.

The BPS is conducted every 8 years and the most recent surveys consist of student cohorts entering college in the 1995–1996, 2003–2004, and 2011–2012 academic years. The BPS collects a variety of data including demographic characteristics, school and work experience, and degree attainment. We obtain a license from the NCES to employ the 2011–2012 cohort (hereafter, BPS12), which is supplemented with the complete history of repayment options, including fixed amortization, forbearance, deferment, and IDR plans, for each loan from the National Student Loan Data System (NSLDS). We observe the starting and ending dates for each option, and when an option is switched. We have these NSLDS data since loan origination up to December 2017. We analyze repayment options up to June 2017, that is, 6 months before the data end, so that we have a sufficient period to examine how each option affects subsequent loan delinquencies.

As explained inInternet Appendix A, both loan forbearance and deferment can be granted for reasons not directly related to the interest of our study. Because we are interested in repayment assistance aiming to resolve borrowers’ financial distress, we only consider forbearance placed due to students’ financial difficulties verifiable by the loan servicer (“discretionary forbearance”) and deferment due to “unemployment” and “economic hardship.”

We take two steps to verify that students in the BPS12 cohort are representative of general college students. First, we confirm that distribution of students in our BPS sample based on gender and race are comparable to that in an alternative data source: the National Postsecondary Student Aid Study (https://nces.ed.gov/surveys/npsas/).7Second, BPS12 provides survey weights to generate a sample that represents the population of first-time beginning students. These weights account for factors including sampling procedures, nonresponse adjustment, and poststratification adjustment. Detailed construction of the sample weights can be found in the 2012/17 Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study – Data File Documentation, chapter 6.8Our analyses are performed using weighted data, and estimates are weighted for panel analysis.

Our license to use this individually identifiable information requires us to round all sample sizes to the nearest 10. Accordingly, the numbers of observations reported in each table and figure and described in the text throughout are rounded numbers divisible by 10.
article.2 Documenting the Puzzle: Suboptimal Stu:
The federal government offers assistance programs to students unable to maintain the standard 10-year amortization schedule to repay federal loans. Loanforbearanceis designed to provide temporary relief to students facing short-term cash flow constraints. Because interest accrued during the loan forbearance period is capitalized, the size and duration of student debt increase when loan forbearance is used over long time periods. In contrast,IDRplans, which set payments as a proportion of discretionary income, stop interest accrual and eventually forgive principal for subsidized loans. Many distressed students qualify for IDR plans with zero-dollar payments. Together, these IDR features are economically preferable to accumulating interest in loan forbearance over long periods. Like IDR,defermentof subsidized loans provides a moratorium on repayment and interest accrual (seeInternet Appendix A). Servicer reps are expected by the DOE to discuss assistance options with distressed borrowers and assist with the enrollment process.

In this section, we document puzzling evidence that most students who qualify for economically advantageous IDR plans or loan deferment options instead continue with long-term loan forbearance (Table D.1). The repeated use of forbearance is not warranted by students’ income prospects; their forecasted income does not suggest that forbearance is preferable to available IDR plans (Figures 1,D.1, and D.2). We also find that long-term forbearance is associated with increasing loan balances and significantly higher loan delinquency than IDR plans or deferment options (Figure 2).

2.1 Sample selection and take-up of federal assistance programs

Internet Appendix Table D.1illustrates our sample selection and provides summary statistics. We winsorize continuous variables at the 1st and 99th percentiles. We analyze loans, rather than students, because repayment options are decided by servicers at the loan level. The loan history for BPS12 students runs through 2017, by which time 87,430 loans enter payment. We exclude loans already in default because they are ineligible for assistance programs (seeInternet Appendix A).

Of the remaining 78,670 loans, 30,320 (38.5%) enter discretionary forbearance as of December 2017. We record the starting date, ending date, and duration of each round of forbearance. When cumulative forbearance duration reaches 12 months, we label the corresponding round of forbearance as “12-month forbearance.” The ending date of this round marks the point when a student borrower has experienced long-term financial distress (rather than temporary cashflow shortage) and is burdened by significant capitalized accrued interest. Among the 30,320 loans, 6,820 (approximately 23%) reach this point of long-term distress. From these, we exclude loans that resume payments indicating no additional financial hardship. We retain the remaining 2,390 loans (approximately 3% of the 78,670 loans), which experience further distress requiring assistance beyond the 12 months prior forbearance. We then identify loan mismanagement based on the repayment options taken up at this point, that is, whether a loan switches to IDR or deferment, or continues to renew forbearance. Of this sample of loans, 280 switch to IDR plans (180 of which qualify for zero-dollar payments), 80 switch into loan deferment, and 2,030 loans (85%) renew forbearance.9

In the “Continued forbearance” section ofTable D.1, we see that for loans renewing forbearance, the averageDays of prior forbearanceis already 485 days. TheDays since last forbearanceindicates that forbearance is renewed after an average 99 days, and theDays of continuing forbearanceindicates that the new rounds of forbearance have an average length of another 273 days. These variables have ball-park similar averages for the groups that switch toIncome-driven repayment (IDR)orDeferment. In the “Income-driven repayment (IDR)” section ofTable D.1, we see that loans switching into IDR plans require $9.72 monthly payment, on average, reflecting distressed borrowers’ low discretionary income. We do not reportDays of IDRsince IDR plans continue until the loan is repaid, forgiven, or switched to an alternative option.

2.2 Accounting for students’ prospective income

Repeated student loan forbearance does not necessarily indicate suboptimal loan management for all students. Certain IDR subprograms, including IBR and PAYE plans (seeTable A.1), can disqualify borrowers’ IDR eligibility and interest moratorium if their earnings grow to the point that scheduled IDR payments (a proportion of discretionary income) surpass the payment required by the standard 10-year amortization. In this case, high-income students revert to the original 10-year plan. Higher monthly payments under the 10-year plan retire debt sooner, but borrowers may prefer slower payments to smooth income over time. Thus, if students anticipate high-income growth, then they may be indifferent between IDR and forbearance or even prefer forbearance.

Our data allow us to test whether the repeated long-term forbearance reflects such rational concerns regarding potential income growth. Because we observe loan-level origination amount, interest rate, student earnings, and occupation, we can estimate the rate at which prospective income must grow for IDR payment to surpass the 10-year standard payment and thus, how likely the potential IDR disqualification would occur.

We do so in three steps. Using loans that continue in forbearance after a minimum of 12 months, we compare their hypothetical outcomes had they instead been switched to an IDR plan. For each forbearance-renewing loan, we obtain the initial loan amount and interest rate to calculate the monthly payment under standard 10-year amortization. We then estimate the payment had the loan switched to an IDR plan based on the student’s gross income (annual job earnings) provided in BPS12.10Applying information on annual poverty thresholds from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), we then obtain the hypothetical IDR payment using 10%–15% of the student’s discretionary income (i.e., gross income in excess of 150% of poverty lines).11

Panel A ofFigure 1compares an average loan monthly payment under the standard 10-year plan versus an IDR plan, based on the borrower’scurrentincome level. Because poverty thresholds vary with family size, we plot the IDR payment for families of 1 to 5 people. In the interest of space,Figure 1compares the standard plan to an IDR payment requiring 10% of discretionary income, though both panels are isomorphic using IDR plans requiring 15% of discretionary income. In all scenarios, students would have paid a significantly lower monthly payment under IDR plans ranging from approximately $2 to $27 (solid line representing the lefty-axis) compared to the $147 standard 10-year repayment (dashed line representing the righty-axis). Students’ current income does not indicate that IDR payments would surpass the 10-year standard payment and thus disqualify IDR plans.12

In the second and third steps, we consider students’prospectiveincome growth. We estimate the level of future income that would result in an IDR payment surpassing the 10-year standard and trigger IDR disqualification. We then estimate how likely distressed sample students are to achieve this higher level of income.Figure 1, panel B, plots students’ average hypothetical annual income (solid line) resulting in IDR payments exceeding the standard 10-year payment. Hypothetical income is calculated for each loan under different borrower family size and averaged across all forbearance-renewing loans. For comparison, the dashed line represents the average of these borrowers’ actual annual income. Even in the most conservative case (a family size of one person), the hypothetical income required for IDR payments to exceed the standard payment is approximately $97,500, which more than triples students’ current income level (approximately $28,300). In untabulated analysis assuming IDR payments of 15% of discretionary income, the hypothetical income required for IDR payments to exceed the standard more than doubles students’ current income level. We believe such income growth is unlikely in the near term among these distressed students.

We test this belief with data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). First, we observe that in the relevant period (2016–2020), the average annual growth rate in nominal median earnings is approximately 4.6% (4.2%; 3.3%) for full-time workers of 20–24 (25–54; 55+) years of age (see BLS Current Population Survey). Next, we calculate the proportion of loans in our sample whose borrowers plausibly achieve the “super growth” in income estimated inFigure 1, panel B. Specifically, the BPS12 provides each student’s six-digit occupation code as of 2017. We then obtain detailed annual wage information for each occupation along with expected wage growth rates from the Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics of the BLS.13Using these growth rates, we estimate each student’s future salary and whether he/she achieves the required “super growth.”Internet Appendix Figure D.2plots under each scenario the proportion of sample loans whose borrower expected income in the next 3 years meets such growth. Under most scenarios the proportion is less than 8%. In the most conservative case (one-person family with IDR payments of 15% of discretionary income) the proportion is less than 12%. As such, concern over future income resulting in IDR disqualification represents a statistically unlikely event.

Overall, we conclude fromFigures 1,D.1, and D.2that students’ re-enrollment in long-term forbearance is unlikely warranted by prospective income growth and more likely reflects suboptimal loan management. To further ensure no ambiguity in this interpretation, we filter our sample in two additional steps. In our baseline analyses we keep only the subset of loans that are federally subsidized, for which student borrowers are not responsible for underpaid interest during the IDR or deferment period.14Of sample loans that switch to IDR plans, we retain only the subset that require zero-dollar monthly payments. These filters ensure that all three assistance programs (IDR plans, loan deferment and continued forbearance) stop monthly payments, but only continued forbearance accrues interest and increases the size and duration of student debt. These steps result in a sample of 1,130 loans serviced by Great Lakes, Navient, FedLoan Servicing, and Nelnet (described inInternet Appendix Aas the Big-4).

2.3 Loan balance evolution and delinquency under different repayment plans

InFigure 2, we establish empirically that (1) long-term forbearance increases the size of students’ debt and (2) continuation of long-term forbearance is associated with higher delinquency rates. In Panel A, we compare the balances of loans switched to IDR or deferment and loans that continue with long-term loan forbearance normalizing initial balances to $100. After a minimum 12-month prior forbearance, resultant balances for both groups of loans reach about 105% of the original amounts borrowed. The added 5% is significant given long-term financial distress. As such, a separation in the path forward (switching or not) may lead to significantly different outcomes. Indeed, the subsequent delinquency rate among loans switched (to IDR or deferment) is approximately 30%. In contrast, after additional rounds of continued forbearance (increasing loan size further) the subsequent delinquency rate is over 70%.

Panel B ofFigure 2plots the evolution in loan balances over time, after resetting the time of separation (when loan balances were approximately 105% of original for both groups) to “100%” for ease of interpretation. We observe from the dashed line that loan balances diminish over time for the group in IDR or deferment plans, while loan balances continue to grow as accrued interest is capitalized for the group continuing in forbearance. By the 36th month following the time of separation, nonswitched loans see a nearly 2% increase in balance on top of the 5% surplus balance at the point of separation.

Overall, Section 2 establishes the puzzle that most distressed student borrowers receive repeated rounds of harmful forbearance rather than switching into IDR plans or loan deferment and that doing so more likely reflects loan mismanagement rather than rational expectation related to IDR disqualification due to high growth in future income.
article.3 Who Mismanages Student Loans?:
3.1 Baseline results

In this section, we analyze the demographic variation in student loan management.Figure 3visualizes the association between loan management and borrower demographics. It describes the proportion of loans, by borrower gender and race, that receive different repayment options after a minimum of 12-month prior forbearance. The bars surrounding each column represent 10% and 90% confidence intervals. Female borrowers are less likely than males to renew forbearance (89.8% vs. 97.8%); they are more likely to switch to either IDR plans (7.7% vs. 1.1%) or loan deferment (3.4% vs. 1.2%). White borrowers are less likely than non-white students to repeat forbearance (87.8% vs. 96.1%) and more likely to switch to IDR plans (8.7% vs. 2.9%) or loan deferment (4.8% vs. 1.1%). A similar difference is seen for white female borrowers versus others.

We formalize the graphical analyses in a multivariate regression framework and estimate the following model:

Equation (1)is a cross-sectional regression, whereiindicates a student loan. It is estimated in the conditional sample of 1,130 subsidized loans described in Section 2. Each of these loans has a minimum 12-month prior forbearance and qualifies for continued distress assistance in the form of IDR plans requiring zero-dollar monthly payment, loan deferment, or continued forbearance. The dependent variableSwitchis an indicator that equals one if the loan switches to either IDR or deferment and equals zero for continued forbearance. We estimate linear probability models which generate more precise estimates of marginal effects (Angrist and Pischke 2008) and allow us to include various fixed effects. We employ two-way clustered standard errors at the student and academic institution level.15

3.2 Financial literacy

Table 1displays the results from the baseline regression model. Prior research has shown that minority students are historically underrepresented in business and finance (e.g., Adams, Barber, and Odean 2016; U.S. DOE 2017) or science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) fields (e.g.,Beede et al. 2011a,b), and that delinquency rates are lower among students with formal finance education (e.g.,Brown et al. 2016). As such, underrepresented students may not fully appreciate the magnitude of financial disadvantage associated with loan forbearance. We therefore account for students’ financial literacy inTable 1, panel A.

In column 1, we report a parsimonious specification including onlyFemaleandWhiteas the independent variables. We add financial literacy variables starting in column 2.Financial acumenis an indicator taking value of one for approximately 40% of sample students who correctly answer at least two of the following questions: “whether a single company’s stock provides a safer return than a stock mutual fund,” “how the future purchasing power of savings changes when the interest rate is lower than the rate of inflation,” and “the total payoff from savings of $100 in five years at an annual interest rate of 5%” (Lusardi and Mitchell 2008).

In column 2, students with such financial acumen are over 8% more likely to switch from forbearance, significant at the 5% level. Comparing column 2 with column 1, financial acumen increases the coefficient ofFemaleby almost 35% (from 0.083 to 0.112). Because female students are historically less financially literate (U.S. DOE 2017), controlling for the element of financial literacy strengthens the positive relation betweenFemaleandSwitch. On the other hand, financial acumen slightly decreases the coefficient ofWhite(from 0.086 to 0.084), consistent with white students typically exhibiting greater financial literacy. However, this control does not subsume the relation betweenWhiteandSwitch.

In column 3, we add students’ major, degree attainment, and parental education level. Students studying business, science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (Business/STEM major) are more likely to switch into advantageous IDR or deferment. Students who attain a degree as of 2017 (Degree) are also more likely to switch, though the magnitude of this difference is smaller.Parents collegeis a binary indicator for whether a student’s parents receive a college degree and has an insignificant effect onSwitch. In column 4, we include students’ family financial standing as an alternative proxy for financial literacy. We also include the average unemployment rate (Unemployment)and average household income of students’ origin location (Family income) reported by the BPS12. Neither is significantly related toSwitch.

In column 5, we include fixed effects for employer industries and an indicator for whether a student works in a finance-related occupation categorized by the BLS;Finance occupationtakes value of one for the “Business & Finance Operation Specialists” category including management analysts, financial analysts, and accountants. Occupation and employer data are available for employed students as of 2017, thus reducing sample size. Finance occupation has the expected but insignificant effect onSwitch.

3.3 Other characteristics

In panel B ofTable 1, we further account for borrower and institution characteristics. In column 1, we include students’ age when first enrolled (Age), indicators for enrollment in a historically Black college (Black college), veteran status (Veteran), marital status (Married), and fixed effects for university selectivity (1, very selective; 2, moderate; 3, minimal; 4, open admission; 5, not a 4-year institution) and for the number of borrower risk factors estimated by BPS12 (e.g., student has dependents or lacks a high school diploma). In column 2, we add fixed effects for field of study and accordingly drop the indicatorBusiness/STEM major.Field of study FEindicates one of 11 fields of study, as categorized in the BPS12. In columns 3 and 4, we add loan origination year fixed effects and repayment year fixed effects (indicating the calendar year when a loan starts the repayment plan to continue loan forbearance or switch to IDR or deferment). In column 5, we add loan servicer fixed effects to control for variation in policy across loan servicers as inAgarwal et al. (2017a). We observe that female students are over 15% more likely to switch out of high-cost forbearance than males and white students are over 8% more likely to switch than non-white peers.

Columns 6 and 7 replicate columns 4 and 5 after replacing the indicators for student gender and race with the term indicating whether the student borrower is a white female. The magnitude of this coefficient is higher than the coefficient for eitherFemaleorWhite, indicating that white female students are over 16% more likely to switch out of continued long-term forbearance than other student demographics.

3.4 Decomposing the effect of gender and race

InFigure 4, we decompose the effect of gender and race by examining pairwise combinations. The left (lighter) section plots the coefficients forFemale, conditioning onWhite=1andWhite=0separately. The hollow and solid points represent specifications with different controls, corresponding to columns 4 and 5 ofTable 1, panel B. Bars surrounding each point represent the 10% and 90% confidence intervals. The coefficients forFemaleare about 0.20 whenWhite=1but reduce to about 0.07 whenWhite=0(marginally significant at 10%). The right (darker) section plots the coefficients forWhite. They are about 0.12 whenFemale=1but indistinguishable from zero whenFemale=0⁠.

As such, while the positive effect ofFemaleis mitigated byWhite=0(left section), the effectWhiteof is completely muted byFemale=0(right section). This contrast implies that for student preference to explain the results (selection-based explanations), one must assume that such preference varies not only by gender or race but also by the interaction of the two. The gender and race variation therefore does not seem to be driven by student preference.

3.5 Robustness tests on variation across gender and race

Analogous to the discussion of future income growth in Section 2.2, in the context of gender and race variation, a concern is that male students historically enjoy higher income growth than females, on average, and thus may rationally prefer forbearance more than females. This difference in expected income growth may explain why female students are more likely to switch.

We test whether our baseline results inTable 1are attributable to concerns (of male borrowers) about future ineligibility of IDR by constructing a subsample of loans that are not subject to these concerns. If our baseline results (switching rates vary by demographics) hold in this subsample, then our results are not driven by potential IDR disqualification associated with income growth.

We construct this subsample in two steps. Among loans switching to IDR (switchers), we keep only the REPAYE plan, which does not disqualify borrowers’ IDR eligibility or recapitalize interest due to earnings growth. Among loans renewing long-term forbearance (nonswitchers), we remove the proportion whose borrower income meets the super growth required to trigger higher payments under IDR than under the standard plan (as identified inFigure D.2). These filters ensure thatneitherswitchers nor nonswitchers has legitimate concerns related to prospective income growth and potential IDR disqualification. We tabulate our results for this subsample inTable 2. The coefficients forFemaleandWhitehave comparable magnitudes as those inTable 1.

Next, we use unsubsidized loans as a basis for comparison and construct another test of IDR disqualification. Unsubsidized loans are excluded inTables 1and2because they do not enjoy interest moratorium under IDR or deferment like subsidized loans do. In this case, we should observe that peer students (female) are relatively more likely (than male) to switch to IDR or deferment for unsubsidized loans compared to the case of subsidized loans.

Based on results fromFigures 1,D.1, and D.2andTables 1–3, we conclude that the repeated renewal of long-term forbearance among distressed students, particularly among male and non-white students, does not seem to be warranted by anticipated income growth; it more likely reflects suboptimal loan management.
article.4 Why Do Students Mismanage Loans?:
We consider potential explanations for the puzzling results so far. We ask: Why might students fail to switch their loans out of costly long-term forbearance? Why is the likelihood of switching associated with student demographics? Who, if anyone, benefits from this systematic failure to switch? The CFPB lawsuit discussed in theIntroductionalleges that borrowers are steered by loan servicers into forbearance against their best interest, suggesting differential treatment from servicers. We consider a treatment-based explanation in a few steps. We first establish that student loans are assigned to loan servicers in a process largely exogenous to borrowers; yet there is significant variation in loan switching rates (away from harmful long-term forbearance) across loan servicers. We then document that such variation is mostly explained by proxies for servicer-level policies; borrower characteristics explain only a small part of this variation. We lastly discuss the incentives of servicers contributing to differential treatment and provide suggestive evidence on how servicer treatment can lead to the gender and race variation in loan mismanagement.

4.1 Background of student loan assignment

The DOE assigns loans to servicers when funds are initially distributed. Before 2015, new loans are assigned to the Big-4 servicers (seeInternet Appendix A.1) based on relative performance. Semiannually, the DOE divides each servicer’s performance score by the sum of scores across servicers, allocating these ratios of new loans in the next six months.16After a loan is assigned, the borrower has limited ability to switch servicers. The two ways a student can potentially do so is to (1) refinance the federal loan with a private loan or (2) consolidate federal loans into a new loan, in which case the consolidated loan is reassigned by the DOE (likely to the same servicer).17

This DOE servicer assignment is designed to be exogenous to individual student borrowers; servicers do not select borrowers based on demographics or risk factors.Internet Appendix Figure D.4andTable 4verify this is the case.Figure D.4presents borrower and loan characteristics across the Big-4 servicers. Because servicer assignment precedes borrowers’ future financial standing or repayment, we perform this analysis using the unconditional sample including all loans in the BPS12 cohort. To alleviate administrative cost, each student’s subsequent loans are assigned to the same servicer as his/her first loan.18We thus capture servicer assignment based on the initial loan to avoid oversampling. InFigure D.4, we observe no significant differences across servicers in the percentage of female or white borrowers, average borrower age, or average loan amount.

4.2 Variation in switching rates

Despite the evidence that the DOE allocates student borrowers similarly across loan servicers, we observe large variation in switching rates across servicers.Table 5, panel A, column 1, reports for each servicer the percentage of loans switched to IDR plans or deferment following a minimal 12-month prior forbearance (i.e., the mean ofSwitch). During our sample period, less than 2% of loans serviced by Navient are switched to IDR plans or deferment; over 98% of students burdened by at least 12 months of accrued interest during forbearance are re-enrolled for continued forbearance. FedLoan Servicing and Nelnet switch about 6.6% and 15.3% students out of long-term forbearance. Great Lakes switches almost 16.5%.20

Accounting for sampling weights, the outstanding loan balance of our sample students is approximately $119.8 million. The interest rate for subsidized federal student loans during the 2011–2012 academic year is approximately 3.48% APR. If all servicers exhibited Navient’s 1.8% switching rate, then the accrued interest for loans continuing in forbearance would amount to approximately $8.8 million over the 2-year average duration of forbearance (Table D.1). If instead all servicers exhibited Great Lakes’ 16.5% switching rate, then accrued interest for loans remaining in forbearance would be approximately $7.5 million, or over $1.3 million in savings. If the DOE further improves the switching rate to 61% (a rate documented by Mueller and Yannelis (2022) using Navient’s field experiment) then the savings would increase to $5.3 million.

In columns 2 to 5 ofTable 5, we see that switching rates across servicers also vary conditional on borrower demographics. For instance, among females in column 2, the switching rate varies from 2.9% (Navient) to 27.9% (Great Lakes); among white borrowers in column 4, the switching rate varies from 1.3% (Navient) to 26.5% (Great Lakes). Because the DOE assigns students similarly across servicers, such conditional variation in switching rates more likely reflects variation in servicer treatment than borrower preference.21

Next, we perform a pairwise Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition and quantify the extent to which borrower demographics explain the variation in switching rates across servicers. Take Great Lakes and Navient—examined in column 1 ofTable 5, panel B—as an example. These servicers have 16.5% and 1.8% switching rates. The Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition estimates Navient’s switching rate substituting Great Lakes’ borrower demographics in place of Navient’s. If Navient’s hypothetical switching rate becomes close to Great Lakes’ actual rate, then we would conclude that borrower demographics explain the switching rate difference between these servicers.

We estimate the Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition in three steps. We regressSwitchon borrower gender and race (FemaleandWhite) separately for loans serviced by Navient and Great Lakes (within-servicer OLS regressions). We then multiply the coefficients forFemaleandWhitefrom the Navient regression by the difference in the mean ofFemaleandWhitebetween Great Lakes’ and Navient’s borrowers. This product estimates the change in Navient’s switching rate when Navient’s borrowers are swapped with Great Lakes’. We then divide this estimated change in switching rate by the actual difference in the two servicers’ switching rates, which indicates the proportion of the difference explained by these borrower characteristics.

Column 1 ofTable 5, panel B, reports these proportions of differences in servicer switching rates explained by student demographics (in %). The number associated withFemale(–1.497) indicates that the proportion of switching rate difference between Navient and Great Lakes “explained” byFemaleis negative 1.497%. The intuition is as follows. In columns 2 and 3 of Panel A, we see that Navient is more likely to switch females than males (2.9% vs. 0.2%). Therefore, if student gender explains why Navient’s switching rate is lower than Great Lakes, then it must be the case that Navient services fewer female students (relative to males) than Great Lakes. However, the opposite is true. Navient services more female students: 59.9% of students assigned to Navient are female compared to 51.4% of students assigned to Great Lakes. As such,Femalecannot explain Navient’s lower switching rate. Rather,Femaleextends the difference, resulting in a negative proportion of the difference “explained” byFemale.Whitecannot explain the difference in switching rates either. The total proportion of difference between these servicers “explained” by gender and race is –1.6%.

In columns 2 and 3 of panel B, we include other borrower characteristics. We add the three financial literacy variables (Financial acumen,Business/STEM major, andDegree) that predict switching outcomes (Table 1) as well as control variables fromTables 1 to 3. Financial literacy explains about 1.8%–2% of the switching rate difference. Other characteristics collectively explain an additional 0.9%. Based on column 3, the cumulative explained proportion reported at the bottom of the table is about 2%. We conclude that borrower characteristics do not explain an economically significant proportion of the switching variation between Navient and Great Lakes.

We draw the same conclusion comparing Nelnet with Navient in columns 4 through 6 and comparing FedLoan with Navient in columns 7 through 9 of panel B. The proportion of variation in switching rates explained by borrower demographics ranges from approximately 2.4% to 5.5% and the proportion explained by all characteristics ranges from approximately 2.5% to 9.7%. InInternet Appendix Table D.3, we report the reverse analyses of these Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition, as well as the decomposition for other pairs exhibiting large switching rate differences. Our conclusion is largely similar. The exogenous assignment of loan servicers, the large variation in switching rate across servicers, and the small proportion of such variation explained by borrower characteristics suggest the presence of treatment effects by loan servicers.

4.3 Servicer incentives

To explore potential treatment effects by loan servicers, we first examine their compensation structure and incentives. Loan servicers receive fixed compensation from the DOE per serviced loan.Figure 5, panel A, presents an example of the contracted compensation, which is constant across servicers, by loan status as of 2014. Loans in repayment (including IDR and standard repayment) receive the highest monthly compensation per loan ($2.85) and loans in default (delinquent for more than 270 days) receive the lowest. Although forbearance appears to earn a lower unit price ($1.05) than IDR or deferment, itadds to, rather than replaces, the revenue from IDR or deferment for loan servicers. Such an addition may in turn incentivize servicers to steer borrowers into forbearance, as alleged by the CFPB lawsuit. We illustrate such an incentive inFigure 5, panels B and C.

Contracted compensation is obtained from the DOE’s website ( https://studentaid.gov/data-center/business-info/contracts/loan-servicing ) and refers to the price per loan serviced by the servicer. Panels B and C delineate servicer revenues under various borrower repayment scenarios. Each node of the charts denotes a possible loan status, and each number in the parentheses of a node presents compensation per loan. These two panels delineate servicer revenues under various scenarios. Panel B considers the case when distressed borrowers are enrolled in IDR plans; it serves as a benchmark for panel C where borrowers are put in forbearance instead. Starting with panel B, several scenarios occur under the IDR plans. In path 1, the student continues with IDR repayment for the next 20–25 years, after which the remaining balance is forgiven. In this case, the servicer collects $2.85 per month during the entire period. In path 2, the student stops the IDR repayment and restarts the standard repayment. This may happen because the student’s income grows sufficiently such that the IDR repayment surpasses the standard 10-year repayment. In this case, the student generates $2.85 for the servicer over the life of the loan. From the servicer’s perspective, paths 1 and 2 generate the same monthly revenue.

The more likely scenarios faced by distressed students are marked by deeper distress rather than recovery, as shown in paths 3 to 5. In path 3, the student becomes delinquent on the loan and the servicer collects $1.23 to $2.11 per loan depending on the duration of delinquency. However, because student loans are generally not dischargeable in bankruptcy (seeYannelis 2020), delinquency does not mark the termination of the loans or the end of servicer revenue. Since principal remains the same or even grows due to accrued interest, the delinquency period simply extends the duration of the student loan and thus adds to the servicer’s total revenue. The nodeDelinquentis presented by a dashed box to denote such an addition. When the student eventually restarts the repayment, as in path 3, servicers are back on track collecting $2.85 per loan.22From the servicer perspective, path 3 looks like paths 1 or 2, with the added revenue earned during the delinquency period. Path 4 builds on path 3 but depicts the case in which the student applies for other assistant programs, such as deferment or forbearance, before restarting the repayment. Following the same intuition, these programs likewise extend the servicer’s revenue stream before the student gets back on track with repayment (generating $2.85 servicer revenue). As such,Deferment/Forbearanceis denoted by a dashed node.

The path undesirable for servicers is illustrated in path 5, where delinquency becomes default. Because defaulted loans can be transferred to the Default Resolution Group (a.k.a. Debt Management and Collection System, a branch of the DOE) this path terminates servicer revenue. This undesirable nodeDefaultis represented by a shaded box.

Figure 5, panel C, demonstrates the loan servicer conflict of interest regarding the use of forbearance, alleged by the CFPB. Under paths 1 and 2, forbearance capitalizes accrued interest thereby extending the duration of the loan and servicer revenue (at the rate of $2.85 per loan once repayment begins) compared to paths 1 and 2 of panel B. Increased loan balances also lead to a higher likelihood of delinquency (Figure 2) and thus a higher high likelihood of path 3 or path 4, generating additional revenue for servicers before students eventually restart payment. Forbearance only hurts servicer revenue when the increased chance of delinquencies eventually leads to default (path 5). From the servicer’s perspective, the trade-off between offering forbearance versus IDR plans depends on this incremental likelihood of path 5. According to the CFPB allegation, the threat of path 5 does not appear to sufficiently discipline all servicers from exploiting the revenue benefit of forbearance.

An alternative contract to improve servicer incentives would increase servicer penalties associated with delinquency and reward servicers for timely loan retirement. The DOE could, for instance, reduce the unit price for delinquent loans from the current 43% to 74% ($1.23 to $2.11) of full rate ($2.85) to a more modest rate and reallocate the resulted savings to bonuses payable to servicers at the retirement of a fully repaid loan or the designated forgiveness of the loan. Doing so would not reduce servicer revenue overall but incentivize servicers to keep loans in repayment (IDR or standard 10-year amortization) and geared toward earliest possible loan retirement. As such, all paths 3 to 5 would be undesirable for servicers who maximize revenue in paths 1 and 2. Given that loan forbearance increases delinquency likelihood and shifts servicer revenue toward paths 3 to 5, such a delinquency penalty could discourage servicers’ repeated use of forbearance.

4.4 Does servicer treatment explain the variation in switching rates?

Next, we examine whether servicer incentives help explain the variation in servicers’ loan switching rates. To capture servicer-level conflict of interest, we followBegley and Purnanandam (2021)and employ complaints filed by students against loan servicers via the CFPB. Intuitively, the scope of complaints regarding a loan servicer should correlate with its incentive and willingness to potentially treat borrowers against their best interest. We perform three analyses based on these servicer complaints and provide the detailed empirical description inInternet Appendix D.1. The key findings of these analyses are as follows.

First, there is significant correlation between switching rates and borrower-adverse servicer policies captured by the intensity of complaints (i.e., the number of complaints against a servicer scaled by the volume of loans it services). The rank of switching rates matches that of complaints: Navient exhibits the lowest switching rate and receives the most complaints; Great Lakes exhibits the highest switching rate and receives the least.23

Second, we employ an Oaxaca-Blinder style decomposition to more formally test the extent to which servicer policies explain the variation in servicer switching rates, in a spirit similar to Section 4.2. We find that servicer policies explain a sizable proportion. From example, between Great Lakes and Navient—exhibiting the highest and lowest switching rates—servicer complaints explain about 78.1% of the switching difference. This magnitude is in stark contrast to the 2% explained by borrower characteristics inTable 5, panel B.

Third,Begley and Purnanandam (2021)further classify complaints based on their resolution. If a complaint is closed withReliefto customers, then it most likely reflects egregious behavior on the part of the financial institution. This type of complaint therefore captures servicers’ materially borrower-adverse policies. Complaints closed withExplanationlikely include issues of a lesser severity and complaintsDismissedmore likely reflect unwarranted criticism. Consistent with this intuition, we find that the explanatory power of servicer complaints on switching rate variation is particularly prominent among those closed withReliefreflecting the most material borrower-adverse policies.

In summary, we conclude from the analyses in Sections 4.2 to 4.4 that borrower characteristics do not drive the switching rate variation across servicers. Instead, servicer treatment seems to play an important role.

4.5 In-group demographic matching between loan servicer representatives and borrowers

The servicer treatment discussed so far can explain the prevalence of long-term loan forbearance generally. Next, we explore whether it further explains the observed demographic variation in loan management. Specifically, we hypothesize that variation in loan management might reflect differential treatment on the part of loan servicer representatives (servicer reps) who ultimately interact with borrowers.

To understand the incentives of individual servicer reps, we gather anecdotal evidence from various sources. Employment reviews on Indeed.com indicate that servicer reps work under pressure to turn calls from borrowers over quickly, allegedly too quickly to allow these reps to assist borrowers in switching their loans into better options, such as IDR plans.24A series of interviews with servicer reps by journalist MollyHensley-Clancy (2017)report that servicer reps chose to “hang up the phone when asked about IDR” given the pressure to turn calls over quickly. Similar issues are noted in the CFPB complaint. In paragraphs 41–44, the complaint specifies that compensation for servicer reps is based on average call time, which incentivizes these reps to push borrowers into forbearance without adequate discussion of alternatives.25

Given that loan servicers’ compensation structure incentivizes reps to achieve rapid call turnover rather than fully assist each caller, we expect the potential for differential treatment across borrowers is not targeted poor treatment of certain demographics, but blanket poor treatment with exceptionally helpful assistance—altruism—provided to borrowers with whom the servicer reps, perhaps subconsciously, feel most empathetic (Bertrand, Chugh, and Mullainathan 2005).

Specifically, we posit that female and white servicer reps are more likely to assist distressed borrowers who are likewise female and white, helping these borrowers switch out of costly long-term forbearance. We provide suggestive evidence for this in-group altruism hypothesis. Because BPS12 does not provide the identities of servicer reps, we followEgan, Matvos, and Seru (2022)and proxy for servicers’ demographic composition. This approach is straightforward: if a loan servicer hires more female (white) servicer reps, then a student calling this servicer is more likely to speak to a female (white) agent.26We capture the demographic composition of servicer reps using hand collected resume data from LinkedIn. For each loan servicer, we identify servicer reps by searching workers whose titles contain “representative,” “agent,” “advisor,” or “specialist.”27We further collect these individuals’ work history, active dates for each job title, corresponding working locations, and the link to their profile photos (if any).

We read each job title and exclude those that appear unrelated to the role of advising students on repayment options (e.g., we exclude “Payroll Specialist” and “Technical Support Specialist”). We further exclude servicer reps whose working locations are not in the United States or not reported, or whose working dates at a loan servicer are not available. These steps result in approximately 2,590 representatives working for Big-4 servicers during our sample period. In most cases, a servicer rep’s gender can be unambiguously identified. We inspect profile photos to identify a servicer rep’s race. This exercise, by nature, is confined to those who have clear photos on LinkedIn. We identify gender for about 99% of servicer reps, and race for about 72% of them. Following these steps, we obtain information on which servicer rep works at a given loan servicer, his/her working years at the servicer, and gender and race classification.

In each year, we calculate the percentage of female and white servicer reps from each loan servicer identified by the resumes. All variables are measured at the yearly level and matched with a loan’s repayment plan year when a borrower contacts the servicer to either switch out of or continue with long-term forbearance. The time-series variation allows us to include servicer fixed effects in later estimation. A higher percentage of female (white) service reps indicates a higher likelihood that a female (white) servicer rep speaks to a student borrower.

The percentage of female reps ranges 53.9% to 65.4% across servicer-years in our sample, and the percentage of white reps ranges 62.9% to 78%.28To address potential concerns over this limited variation, we assign each percentage to the decile of its distribution, in the spirit ofKuhnen and Melzer (2019). Instead of using the raw percentages, we generate two variables,Female pct.andWhite pct., with values ranging from 1 to 10, denoting the corresponding decile categories of each servicer’s percentage of female or white service reps. We then reestimateEquation (1)including the two new variables, along with their interaction terms with demographic indicators.

Table 6reports the results. In column 1 the variable of interest isFemale pct.×Female (0,1).Female pct.captures the percentage of female servicers and thus the likelihood a student borrower speaks to a female servicer rep.Female (0,1)captures the gender of a student borrower. The positive and significant coefficient for the interaction term suggests that female borrowers are particularly more likely to switch their loans out of forbearance (compared to male borrowers) when they call a loan servicer with a relatively high percentage of female servicer reps.

This result is consistent with the in-group favoritism observed in recent studies (e.g.,Ambrose, Conklin, and Lopez 2021;Egan, Matvos, and Seru 2022;Ewens and Townsend 2020;Duchin, Simutin, and Sosyura 2021). It is also consistent with our conjecture that servicer reps are more likely to give helpful advice and assist distressed borrowers when both the borrower and the servicer rep are female. This result holds when we include servicer fixed effects in column 2. Similarly, in columns 3 and 4 the positive coefficients forWhite pct.×White (0,1)indicate that white borrowers are more likely to switch their loans when they call a servicer rep who is also likely white. The significance of these coefficients, however, is weaker at the 10% level.29

Next, we explore whether in-group altruism interacts with students’ level of financial literacy. To the extent that information from servicer reps is in more need for borrowers with low levels of financial literacy, we expect the effects of gender and race paring to be stronger among these borrowers. On the other hand, borrowers with greater financial literacy are equipped to make decisions regardless of pairing with reps.

We explore this intuition inInternet Appendix Figure D.5. We repeat the analyses inTable 6separately for two subsamples of borrowers, divided by their level of financial literacy. For each borrower, we measure their overall financial literacy using the sum ofFinancial acumen,Business/STEM major, andDegree(seeTable 1). If this sum is two or greater for a borrower, then he/she is considered to have a higher level of financial literacy; otherwise, the borrower has a lower level of financial literacy. In the left (light) section ofFigure D.5, we plot the coefficient ofFemale pct.×Female (0,1)for students with low versus high financial literacy. The regression specification corresponds to column 2 ofTable 6. As predicted, the coefficient is larger (0.029), and thus the effect of gender-based altruism is stronger, for students with lower financial literacy. It becomes insignificant for students with higher financial literacy. The difference in the coefficients between the two groups is significant at the 5% level, with ap-value of .02. In the right (darker) section ofFigure D.5, we find that race-based altruism does not vary significantly with students’ financial literacy. The coefficient forWhite pct.×White (0,1)is 0.021 for low financial literacy and 0.019 for higher financial literacy, which is an insignificant difference.

Overall, the results fromTable 6andFigure D.5are consistent with our conjecture that distressed student borrowers have better loan management outcomes when they are more likely connected to a servicer rep of the same gender and race. Such in-group altruism is more pronounced for those with a lower level of financial literacy. The interaction is stronger when borrowers and servicer reps share a common gender than when they share a common race.

Because our data do not allow direct observation of service rep identity, we interpret this evidence as suggestive and call for future research based on more granular data. To the extent that the DOE incentivizes servicers to improve switching rates, this suggestive evidence commends that servicer reps’ compensation also should be adjusted to reward quality of advice rather than speed of call turnover.

4.6 Selection-based explanation: Aversion to administrative paperwork

After exploring servicer treatment to explain demographic variation in student loan management, in this section we explore alternative explanations based on borrower preferences (selection). We first consider that male and non-white students may fail to switch from long-term forbearance into deferment or IDR plans due to nonpecuniary borrower preferences. In particular, the results from Mueller and Yannelis (2022) indicate that aversion to administrative paperwork is a significant factor in the IDR take-up rate. To the extent that aversion to paperwork varies by student gender and race, the observed demographic variation in student loan outcomes might be attributed to variation in such borrower preferences.

To investigate this explanation, we replicate the baseline results inTable 1after setting aside IDR plans, which require burdensome (12-page) applications. We focus instead on the choice between repeated forbearance versus loan deferment. Both require relatively straight-forward four-page applications. By design, each student has already demonstrated a willingness to complete a four-page application in prior rounds of forbearance. By comparing programs with similar paperwork costs, any remaining variation in borrowers’ failure to switch distressed loans out of forbearance is not likely attributable to borrowers’ aversion to administrative paperwork.

We use a loan matching process to ensure the comparison between forbearance and deferment is performed on an equal footing. For each loan that switches into deferment after the prior 12-month forbearance (which are fewer in number), we match a loan that renews forbearance (which are greater in number) that is granted in the same calendar year-month as the loan in deferment and that has comparable duration (plus or minus maximum 30 days) to the deferred loan. This matching creates a setting in which both repayment options are granted in the same period, are designed to address a similar magnitude of financial hardship (captured by the duration) and require zero-dollar monthly payments. As such, the loan in deferment can be thought of as a counterfactual outcome for the loan that renews forbearance. We tabulate the results inTable 7and confirm the findings inTable 1. Therefore, the variation in borrower aversion to administrative paperwork cannot fully explain the demographic variation in repayment outcomes.

In column 1, we include controls for various borrower characteristics defined inTable 1. Prior literature establishes a significant association between loan default and gender or race. The insignificance of these explanatory variables here is perhaps not surprising given the nature of our sample that includes only borrowers with long-term financial distress (with a minimum of 12 months prior loan forbearance). In column 2, we includeSwitchas the only independent variable. The negative and significant coefficient indicates that loans switched into loan deferment or IDR plans are over 50% less likely to become delinquent than loans that continue in long-term loan forbearance. The magnitude of this coefficient is economically significant.

In columns 3 and 4, we observe that the significance of the coefficient forSwitchis robust to the inclusion of control variables and loan servicer fixed effects. Overall, we conclude fromTable 8that continued long-term loan forbearance increases student loan delinquency. To the extent that continued long-term loan forbearance reflects selection on the part of informed students, the selection is irrational. To the extent that the continued forbearance reflects differential treatment on the part of student loan servicers, this treatment materially affects loan outcomes.

5.2 Robustness tests

InInternet Appendix Table D.7, we perform various robustness tests to our main analyses, including (1) dropping loans serviced by Navient (the servicer at the center of recent CFPB allegations and other lawsuits), (2) alternative definitions of student burdens, and (3) extending the sample to include more than one repayment option following long-term forbearance. Our main findings are robust, and our conclusion is unchanged.
article.6 Conclusion:
Using data on student loan borrowers from the DOE, we document and shed light on the puzzling reality that the majority of financially distressed student borrowers have their loans in disadvantageous repayment plans (long-term forbearance) when eligible for more economically advantageous options. We confirm that this loan mismanagement is associated with higher delinquency rates, provide cross-sectional analysis on which students suboptimally manage their loans, and explore possible explanations for this puzzling behavior.

We find significant demographic variation in student loan management: female and white students are more likely to switch their loans to economically advantageous options rather than continue with costly loan forbearance. Finance literacy, which is likely correlated with student demographics, partially explains the different loan management outcomes but does not subsume the variation by gender or race. Motivated by federal and state allegations against one student loan servicer, we test for the presence of servicer treatment effects. We also test several potential selection-based explanations including aversion to administrative paperwork. Overall, the evidence supports the conclusion that differential treatment on the part of loan servicers plays an important role in student loan management.

Our results suggest an opportunity for improved student loan outcomes (reduced delinquency and earlier loan retirement) by adjusting loan servicer compensation set by the DOE. Specifically, the DOE could reduce servicer compensation for delinquent loans and reallocate the resultant savings to bonuses payable to servicers at the retirement (or designed forgiveness by IDR) of a loan. Doing so would not reduce servicer revenue overall but incentivize servicers to keep loans in repayment (IDR if not standard 10-year amortization) and geared toward earliest possible loan retirement. To the extent that the DOE incentivizes servicers to promote speedier repayment, it seems clear that servicer reps’ compensation also should be adjusted to reward quality of advice rather than speed of call turnover.
